title: Castile & Aragon
subtitle: 1230–1492 · Union, Reconquest and the Catholic Monarchs

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From Ferdinand III to the Catholic Monarchs — the making of Spain

entries.label: Ferdinand III — the Saint who unified Castile and León
entries.hint: Ferdinand III unites the crowns of Castile and León permanently (1230) — then conquers Córdoba (1236), Jaén (1246) and Seville (1248) in rapid succession; canonised in 1671; his tomb in Seville Cathedral contains his sword and a multilingual epitaph in Latin, Castilian, Arabic and Hebrew.
entries.note: Ferdinand III's multilingual epitaph: Cathedral of Seville — the four languages symbolise his multicultural kingdom. His canonisation 1671: Vatican records. Primera Crónica General (Alfonso X, c.1270): documents his campaigns. The unification of Castile and León 1230: documented in royal charters (AHN). Linehan (1993), History and Historians of Medieval Spain: analyses the source tradition.

entries.label: Alfonso X el Sabio — the scholar king
entries.hint: Alfonso X is the most intellectually productive medieval Iberian monarch — he supervises the Siete Partidas (law code), the Cantigas de Santa María (420 songs with musical notation), astronomical tables, and a general history of Spain; his multilingual court includes Christian, Muslim and Jewish scholars.
entries.note: Alfonso X's reign: documented through his own prolific output. The Siete Partidas: multiple manuscripts; Las siete partidas del Rey don Alfonso (BnF and BNE). The Cantigas de Santa María: 4 illuminated manuscripts survive — Real Biblioteca del Monasterio de El Escorial (T.I.1 and B.I.2), BNE and BnF. His astronomical Alfonsine Tables: used in Europe until the 17th century — British Library holds early copies.

entries.label: James I of Aragon — the Mediterranean empire
entries.hint: James I of Aragon conquers the Balearic Islands (1229) and Valencia (1238) — establishing the Crown of Aragon as a Mediterranean power; his own autobiography the Llibre dels Fets is the first royal autobiography in a European vernacular language.
entries.note: Llibre dels Fets (Book of Deeds, c.1270–1276): James I's own autobiography — oldest royal autobiography in a vernacular European language. BNE and Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó hold manuscripts. Smith & Buffery (2003), The Book of Deeds of James I of Aragon: the standard English translation. The Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean expansion: documented in treaty archives.

entries.label: Ferdinand and Isabella — the Catholic Monarchs
entries.hint: Ferdinand of Aragon and Isabella of Castile marry in 1469 — their union doesn't formally merge the two kingdoms but creates a personal union that acts as one; they complete the Reconquista (1492), expel the Jews (1492), and send Columbus (1492); they are the effective founders of unified Spain.
entries.note: The marriage contract 18 Oct 1469: Archivo de la Corona de Aragón — the primary document of the dynastic union. Their joint titulature ('los Reyes Católicos'): conferred by Pope Alexander VI 1496 — Vatican Archives. The Alhambra Decree (31 Mar 1492): AGS. Columbus commission: AGS Patronato Real. Edwards (2000), Ferdinand and Isabella: the standard English biography.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Final reconquest, civil wars and Aragon's Mediterranean campaigns

entries.label: Ferdinand III conquers Córdoba and Seville
entries.hint: Ferdinand III's rapid reconquest of the Guadalquivir valley — Córdoba falls to a night raid on 7 January 1236; Seville after a two-year siege on 23 November 1248; the Great Mosque of Córdoba is consecrated as a cathedral the day it falls; the heart of al-Andalus passes to Christian rule.
entries.note: The fall of Córdoba 7 Jan 1236: Primera Crónica General (Alfonso X). The night raid by Domingo Muñoz: documented — he disguised his men as Moors. The fall of Seville 23 Nov 1248: documented in the Primera Crónica and Arabic sources. Ferdinand III's epitaph in four languages: in situ, Cathedral of Seville. González (1980), Fernando III el Santo: the standard modern biography.

entries.label: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa consequences — Valencia falls
entries.hint: James I of Aragon takes Valencia from its Muslim rulers in 1238 — completing the Aragonese reconquest of the eastern peninsula; Valencia becomes the most populous city of the Crown of Aragon; its Aragonese charter (Furs de Valencia) gives it distinctive legal status.
entries.note: The fall of Valencia 28 Sep 1238: documented in the Llibre dels Fets (James I's own account). The Furs de Valencia (1261): Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — the Valencian law code. The founding of the Kingdom of Valencia as distinct from Aragon: documented in the charter archives. The moriscos who remain in Valencia: documented in subsequent administrative records.

entries.label: Castilian civil war — Pedro I vs Enrique II
entries.hint: The Castilian civil war between King Pedro I ('the Cruel') and his illegitimate half-brother Enrique de Trastámara involves England (Black Prince supports Pedro), France (Du Guesclin supports Enrique) and the Crown of Aragon; Pedro is murdered at Montiel (1369); the Trastámara dynasty takes power.
entries.note: The murder of Pedro I at Montiel 23 Mar 1369: documented in multiple chronicles. The Black Prince's intervention (Battle of Nájera 1367): English chronicles document his role. Froissart's Chronicles: detailed account of the English involvement — BnF. The Trastámara dynasty's subsequent rule: documented in royal archives. Russell (1955), The English Intervention in Spain and Portugal: the standard study.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: The Siete Partidas, Cortes and the development of Iberian law

entries.label: Siete Partidas — Alfonso X's universal law code
entries.hint: Alfonso X's Siete Partidas (Seven-Part Code, c.1265) is the most comprehensive medieval Iberian law code — 2,800 laws in 7 parts covering religion, royal power, nobles, towns, contracts, wills and criminal law; it remains in force in parts of Louisiana and Texas until the 19th century.
entries.note: Siete Partidas (c.1265): BNE holds the primary manuscripts. Issued as law 1348 by Alfonso XI (Ordenamiento de Alcalá). US influence: still referenced in Louisiana courts until 1825 — documented in US legal records. Burns (2001), Las Siete Partidas (University of Pennsylvania): the standard English translation. The code's treatment of Jews and Muslims: explicit legal provisions documented — less restrictive than later legislation.

entries.label: Cortes of Castile and Aragon — early representative assemblies
entries.hint: The Cortes of León (1188) is the earliest documented representative assembly in European history — meeting before the Magna Carta (1215); the Aragonese Corts is even more powerful; both assemblies develop documentary traditions of petitions and royal responses that are primary sources.
entries.note: Cortes of León 1188: UNESCO Memory of the World (2013) — the decrees are the primary documents. The Aragonese Corts: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — extensive documentation. Valdeavellano (1968), Historia de España Vol.I: the standard survey. The León 1188 Cortes' UNESCO inscription: explicitly as 'the first representative parliament in world history'.

entries.label: Spanish Inquisition established — 1478
entries.hint: Ferdinand and Isabella petition Pope Sixtus IV to establish a royal Inquisition — the papal bull of 1 November 1478 authorises it; the Inquisition is under royal (not papal) control; its first auto de fe is held in Seville in 1481; Tomás de Torquemada is appointed Inquisitor General in 1483.
entries.note: Papal bull Exigit sincerae devotionis (1 Nov 1478): Vatican Archives. Torquemada's appointment 1483: documented. The first auto de fe (Seville, 6 Feb 1481): documented in contemporary records. AHN Inquisición: the primary archive for all subsequent proceedings. Kamen (1997), The Spanish Inquisition: the standard modern study — covers this founding period.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Church, Inquisition and the end of religious pluralism

entries.label: Las Huelgas — royal monastery and Cistercian centre
entries.hint: Alfonso VIII founds Las Huelgas monastery in 1187 — it becomes the royal pantheon of Castile (32 kings and queens buried there), a Cistercian intellectual centre, and the holder of an extraordinary archive including the Almohad battle standard captured at Las Navas; its music manuscripts are primary sources for 13th-century polyphony.
entries.note: Extant (partially closed to public). The Las Navas battle standard: in situ at Las Huelgas. The music manuscripts (Códice de Las Huelgas, c.1300): BNE — the largest collection of 13th-century polyphony in Spain. Anderson (1982), The Las Huelgas Manuscript: the standard musicological study. The royal tombs: documented in Gómez-Moreno (1946), El panteón real de Las Huelgas.

entries.label: Samuel Halevi Abulafia — treasurer, synagogue builder
entries.hint: Samuel Halevi Abulafia, treasurer of Pedro I of Castile, builds the El Tránsito synagogue in Toledo (c.1357) — the finest medieval synagogue in Spain; its Hebrew inscriptions praise both God and Pedro I; its survival after the 1391 pogroms and the 1492 expulsion is itself a primary source.
entries.note: El Tránsito synagogue (c.1357): extant, Toledo (Museo Sefardí). The Hebrew inscriptions: translated and documented. Samuel Halevi's subsequent arrest and execution by Pedro I (c.1360): documented in chronicles. Baer (1961), A History of the Jews in Christian Spain Vol.II. The synagogue's conversion to a church after 1492 and its survival: documented. The inscription praising Pedro I alongside God: primary evidence for Jewish-Christian political collaboration.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Gothic cathedrals, the Cantigas and Mudéjar art

entries.label: Mudéjar architecture — Muslim craftsmen for Christian patrons
entries.hint: Mudéjar architecture is produced by Muslim craftsmen working for Christian and Jewish patrons — it fuses Islamic geometric decoration, horseshoe arches and brick construction with Gothic and Romanesque structural forms; it is uniquely Iberian, found nowhere else.
entries.note: UNESCO World Heritage: Mudéjar architecture of Aragon (1986, extended 2001) — towers of Teruel and Zaragoza. The Seville Alcázar (14th c.): extant (UNESCO) — built by Pedro I using Nasrid craftsmen from Granada; the primary monument. The Sinagoga del Tránsito, Toledo: also Mudéjar. Dodds (1990), Architecture and Ideology: the standard study of Mudéjar's cross-cultural meaning.

entries.label: Cantigas de Santa María — Alfonso X's illustrated songbook
entries.hint: The Cantigas de Santa María is 420 poems with musical notation — the largest collection of medieval monophonic songs from any single source; four illuminated manuscripts survive with miniatures documenting 13th-century daily life; the musical notation is the primary source for Iberian medieval music.
entries.note: Four manuscripts: El Escorial T.I.1 (the Florence codex), El Escorial B.I.2, BNE 10069, BnF Esp.361. Katz & Keller (1987), Studies on the Cantigas de Santa María: the standard scholarly collection. The miniatures as social history: Ferreira (2019) uses them as primary sources for musical instruments, clothing and daily life. The musical notation: enables modern performance — documented in recordings.

entries.label: The Llibre Vermell de Montserrat — pilgrimage songs (c.1399)
entries.hint: The Llibre Vermell (Red Book) of Montserrat contains 10 songs for pilgrims to sing or dance at the shrine — the earliest documented use of dancing in a Christian devotional context; the manuscript also contains plague songs written during the Black Death epidemics; it is the oldest extant Catalan music manuscript.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Llibre Vermell de Montserrat (c.1399): Arxiu de l'Abadia de Montserrat. The 10 songs include polyphonic and monophonic pieces. Gómez (1990), La música medieval en España: the standard study. The dancing at shrines: the text explicitly describes pilgrims dancing in the nave — 'because the pilgrims are accustomed to watch at night in the church'. The plague songs: composed c.1348–1350 — documented by internal references.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Alfonso X's scriptorium and the intellectual synthesis

entries.label: Alfonsine Tables — astronomy in three languages
entries.hint: Alfonso X commissions revised astronomical tables — the Alfonsine Tables — from a team of Jewish and Muslim astronomers working in Toledo; compiled in Arabic, translated to Castilian, later to Latin; used by European astronomers including Copernicus until the 17th century.
entries.note: Alfonsine Tables (c.1270): compiled by Isaac ibn Sid and Judah ben Moses ha-Cohen. The Paris edition 1483: the standard Latin version. Copernicus used them: documented in his notes. Chabás & Goldstein (2003), The Alfonsine Tables of Toledo: the standard modern study. The collaboration of Jewish and Muslim astronomers under a Christian king: a primary example of convivencia in intellectual production.

entries.label: Ramon Llull — missionary philosophy and the Art
entries.hint: Ramon Llull is the first European philosopher to write systematic philosophy in a vernacular language (Catalan) — his Ars Magna proposes a logical machine for generating theological arguments; he learns Arabic, visits North Africa on three missionary journeys, and is reportedly stoned to death in Tunis.
entries.note: Ars Magna (c.1274): Latin and Catalan manuscripts — BnF and Archivo de la Corona d'Aragó. Llull's Catalan works: the first philosophical corpus in Catalan. Bonner (2007), The Art and Logic of Ramon Llull: the standard English study. His death in Tunis c.1316: documented in contemporary sources (disputed). His influence on later logic and computing: documented — Leibniz credited him as an influence.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Wool, silver and the economy of medieval Castile

entries.label: The Mesta — Castilian wool and the transhumance economy
entries.hint: Alfonso X charters the Mesta (the shepherds' guild) in 1273 — organising the seasonal migration of up to 2 million merino sheep between summer pastures in the north and winter pastures in the south; Castilian wool drives the Flemish textile industry and becomes the engine of the medieval Castilian economy.
entries.note: Mesta charter 1273: AGS and AHN. Klein (1920), The Mesta: A Study in Spanish Economic History 1273–1836: the standard study. The merino sheep numbers: documented in 14th-c. fiscal records. The Castile-Flanders wool trade: documented in Bruges merchant archives and Castilian royal customs records. The Mesta's long-term impact on Castilian agriculture: documented in subsequent land-use surveys.

entries.label: Black Death in Iberia — 1348 and the Jewish pogroms
entries.loc: Catalonia; Castile; Aragon (arrival via Mediterranean ports)
entries.hint: The Black Death reaches Iberia via Catalan ports in 1348 — killing an estimated 30–40% of the population; Jews are blamed for poisoning wells and massacred (Tàrrega 1348; Barcelona 1348); the pogroms of 1348 foreshadow and directly cause the larger pogroms of 1391.
entries.note: The Tàrrega pogrom 1348: documented in contemporary municipal records and now genetically confirmed — MDPI study (March 2026) using DNA from six mass graves at the Roquetes necropolis, Tàrrega. The Barcelona pogrom 1348: documented in municipal archives (Arxiu Municipal de Barcelona). Nirenberg (1996), Communities of Violence: the standard study of 14th-century Iberian pogroms. The Black Death mortality: Benedictow (2004), The Black Death 1346–1353, estimates 30–40% for Iberia.

entries.label: Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean empire — the Catalan Atlas (1375)
entries.hint: The Catalan Atlas (1375) made by Abraham Cresques in Mallorca is the most detailed world map of the 14th century — showing the Silk Road, West African gold kingdoms, and the Indian Ocean; it is the product of the Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean mercantile intelligence.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Catalan Atlas (1375): BnF (Manuscrit espagnol 30) — digitised. Made by Abraham Cresques, a Jewish cartographer in Mallorca. The detail of West Africa (including Mali's gold): drawn from traveller accounts. Pujades (2007), Les cartes portolanes: the standard study. The Crown of Aragon's Mediterranean trade documentation: Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó — extensive merchant records.

entries.label: Iberian-Genoese alliance — trade and Columbus's context
entries.hint: Genoese merchants are embedded in Castilian and Aragonese trade networks throughout the 14th and 15th centuries — providing credit, maritime expertise and commercial connections; Christopher Columbus is a Genoese working for Castile; his voyage is the product of this long Ibero-Genoese commercial partnership.
entries.note: Genoese merchant records in Seville: Archivo Histórico Provincial de Sevilla. The Genoese colony in Seville: documented from the 13th century. Columbus's Genoese origins: documented in notarial records from Genoa. Phillips (1988), Columbus: the standard biography, documents the Genoese-Castilian commercial world. The connection between the Genoese network and the 1492 voyage: Fernández-Armesto (1991), Columbus.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: The Catholic Monarchs and the birth of the global world

entries.label: 1492 — the year Spain makes the modern world
entries.loc: Granada; Palos de Moguer; Atlantic Ocean
entries.hint: Three events in 1492 authorised by Ferdinand and Isabella from the Alhambra: Granada falls (2 January), Jews expelled (31 March), Columbus departs (3 August); the same year Nebrija publishes the first Castilian grammar — the tools of empire, religious homogeneity and overseas expansion all coincide.
entries.note: Granada's fall 2 Jan 1492: documented. Alhambra Decree 31 Mar 1492: AGS. Columbus's departure 3 Aug 1492: his journal (BNF). Nebrija's Gramática castellana (1492): BNE — first grammar of a modern European vernacular language. Elliot (2002), Imperial Spain 1469–1716: the standard modern study of the Catholic Monarchs' legacy. The connection between language standardisation and empire: Nebrija's preface explicitly argues grammar is the 'companion of empire'.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: Isabella I — queen regnant and architect of Spain
entries.hint: Isabella I of Castile is crowned in Segovia on 13 December 1474 — ruling in her own right; she initiates the Inquisition, completes the Reconquista, expels the Jews, and authorises Columbus; she is the most consequential woman in the founding of the modern world and the primary political actor behind every 1492 event.
entries.note: Isabella's coronation 13 Dec 1474: Segovia — documented in contemporary chronicles. Her personal correspondence: AGS — documented. Liss (1992), Isabel the Queen: the standard modern biography. The Granada capitulations and Alhambra Decree: both issued jointly but Isabella's role documented as dominant in Spanish sources. The contrast with her husband: Ferdinand handled Aragon; Isabella handled Castile — documented in their separate chancelleries.

entries.label: The conversos — forced converts and their impossible position
entries.hint: The conversos (Jewish converts to Christianity, mostly forced) are the central social problem of 15th-century Castile — too Christian to be Jewish, too recently Jewish to be trusted as Christians; the Inquisition is created specifically to investigate their sincerity; the blood purity statutes (limpieza de sangre) later bar them from office.
entries.note: The converso population: Beinart (2002) estimates 100,000+ forced conversions in 1391. The Inquisition's primary targets: AHN Inquisición records show conversos as the dominant category in 15th-c. proceedings. The Toledo blood purity statute (1449): the first such statute — documented in AHN (see Visigothic Spain folio connection). Netanyahu (1995), The Origins of the Inquisition: the foundational study of the converso problem. This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT: the creation of a new intermediate category (converso) that satisfies neither Christian nor Jewish frameworks.

entries.label: African slavery in medieval Iberia — the Valencian slave market
entries.hint: The Crown of Aragon operates slave markets in Valencia and Barcelona from the 14th century — trading primarily in sub-Saharan Africans, Moors, and enslaved people from the Black Sea region; the Valencian notarial records are among the best-documented medieval European slave trade archives.
entries.note: Valencian notarial records documenting slave sales: Arxiu del Regne de València — published in Verlinden (1955), L'Esclavage dans l'Europe médiévale Vol.I. The Barcelona slave market: documented in Arxiu de la Corona d'Aragó. Blumenthal (2009), Enemies and Familiars: Slavery and Mastery in Fifteenth-Century Valencia: the standard modern study using the notarial records. The scale: Blumenthal documents 10,000+ slave sales in Valencia alone 1375–1425.

events.l: Catalan Atlas — world map from Mallorca
